manifest.description: Blueprint-compiled draft dispatch pack for math.abst.binary-operation (Phase 1 front-end; DRAFT only)

assets.title: math.abst.binary-operation ‚ÄĒ cognitive map
assets.content:
### Target Understanding
A **binary operation** on a set S is a function ‚ąó: S √ó S ‚Üí S that assigns to every ordered pair
(a, b) ‚ąą S √ó S exactly one element a‚ąób ‚ąą S.  The critical constraint is **closure**: the result
a‚ąób must lie in S for every a, b ‚ąą S.  Associativity, commutativity, the existence of an identity
element, and the existence of inverses are **additional properties** an operation may or may not
have ‚ÄĒ they are not part of the definition of a binary operation.

### Conceptual Layers (C ‚Üí P ‚Üí A)
| Layer | Content |
|-------|---------|
| **Concrete (C)** | Familiar operations on ‚Ą§: addition, subtraction, multiplication; checking closure by trying specific pairs |
| **Pictorial (P)** | Cayley (operation) table for a small finite set; arrow diagram of ‚ąó: S√óS‚ÜíS |
| **Abstract (A)** | Formal definition: ‚ąó: S√óS‚ÜíS total and well-defined; the four extra properties (associativity, commutativity, identity, inverse) as separate, optional attributes |

### Prerequisite Knowledge (from KG)
- **math.found.function-set-theoretic** ‚ÄĒ a function is a total, well-defined map from domain to codomain; ordered pairs; Cartesian product S√óS

### Key Distinctions
| Property | Definition | Holds for + on ‚Ą§? | Holds for ‚ąí on ‚Ą§? | Holds for √ó on ‚Ąē? |
|----------|------------|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|
| **Closure** | a‚ąób ‚ąą S for all a,b | ‚úď | ‚úď | ‚úď |
| **Commutativity** | a‚ąób = b‚ąóa | ‚úď | ‚úó (3‚ąí5 ‚Č† 5‚ąí3) | ‚úď |
| **Associativity** | (a‚ąób)‚ąóc = a‚ąó(b‚ąóc) | ‚úď | ‚úó | ‚úď |
| **Identity** | ‚ąÉe: a‚ąóe=e‚ąóa=a | ‚úď (e=0) | ‚úó | ‚úď (e=1) |
| **Inverses** | ‚ąÄa ‚ąÉa‚ĀĽ¬Ļ: a‚ąóa‚ĀĽ¬Ļ=e | ‚úď (‚ąía) | n/a | ‚úó (no 1/a in ‚Ąē) |

---

<!-- COMPONENT 2: MISCONCEPTION REGISTRY -->

assets.title: CLOSURE-VIOLATION
assets.content: | MC-1 | CLOSURE-VIOLATION | Accepts subtraction on ‚Ąē as a binary operation; doesn't check 2‚ąí5=‚ąí3‚ąČ‚Ąē | Treats any "rule" on a set as a binary operation without verifying that outputs stay in the set | **FOUNDATIONAL** |

assets.title: COMMUTATIVITY-REQUIRED
assets.content: | MC-2 | COMMUTATIVITY-REQUIRED | Claims matrix multiplication or function composition "can't be a binary operation" because they're not commutative | Conflates commutativity (an optional extra property) with the definition of binary operation | Secondary |

assets.title: ARITHMETIC-ONLY
assets.content: | MC-3 | ARITHMETIC-ONLY | Doesn't recognise set intersection, function composition, string concatenation, or XOR as binary operations | Mental model of "operation" anchored on +, ‚ąí, √ó, √∑ | Secondary |

assets.title: Entry: The Definition via Concrete Examples (P03 + P11 + P49)
assets.content: {"id":"TA-A01","title":"Entry: The Definition via Concrete Examples (P03 + P11 + P49)","primitives":["P03","P11","P49"],"addresses":["MC-1"]}

assets.title: MC-1 Gate: Closure Check (P41 + P64 + P49)
Properties: Contrast Commutative vs Non-Commutative (P06 + P49)
Terminal Mastery Gate (P91)
assets.content:
### TA-A04 ¬∑ Terminal Mastery Gate (P91)

```
P91 = P77 ‚Üí P55 ‚Üí P76 ‚Üí P55 ‚Üí P75 ‚Üí P55 ‚Üí P74 ‚Üí P55 ‚Üí P78
```

**[P77 ‚ÄĒ MULTI-PROBLEM SET]**  (4 questions)

**Q1.** Which is a binary operation on ‚Ąē = {1, 2, 3, ‚Ä¶}?
(A) a ‚ąó b = |a ‚ąí b| (absolute difference)
(B) a ‚ąó b = a ‚ąí b
(C) a ‚ąó b = a / b
(D) None of the above

*(Target: A ‚ÄĒ |a ‚ąí b| is always a non-negative integer, but wait: |2‚ąí5|=3 ‚ąą ‚Ąē, |5‚ąí5|=0 ‚ąČ ‚Ąē if ‚Ąē={1,2,3,...}. Actually |a-b|=0 when a=b, and 0‚ąČ‚Ąē under the convention ‚Ąē={1,2,...}. So A also fails! Under ‚Ąē = {0,1,2,...} (including zero), A works. This is ambiguous. Let me change Q1 to avoid this.)*

Revised Q1: Which is a binary operation on ‚Ą§ (all integers)?
(A) a ‚ąó b = a + b ‚ąí 2
(B) a ‚ąó b = a / b
(C) a ‚ąó b = ‚ąö(a + b)
(D) a ‚ąó b = the larger of a and b

*(Target: A and D are both valid on ‚Ą§ ‚ÄĒ but the question asks for a single answer. Let me say "select all that apply." Actually for simplicity: A gives an integer (closed ‚úď); B may not give integer (1/3 ‚ąČ ‚Ą§, fails); C may not give integer (‚ąö3 ‚ąČ ‚Ą§, fails); D max(a,b) is always an integer ‚úď. So A and D. Let me make this a two-answer question.)*

Let me redesign Q1 more cleanly:

**Q1.** Decide: Is a ‚ąó b = a + b ‚ąí 2 a binary operation on ‚Ą§?  Justify in one sentence.

*(Target: Yes ‚ÄĒ a+b‚ąí2 is always an integer when a,b ‚ąą ‚Ą§. Closure holds. ‚úď)*

**Q2.** Is matrix multiplication a binary operation on the set of all 2√ó2 real matrices?  Is it commutative?

*(Target: Yes, binary operation (closed: 2√ó2 times 2√ó2 = 2√ó2). Not commutative: give example or state AB ‚Č† BA in general.)*

**Q3.** Let S = {‚ąí1, 0, 1} and define a ‚ąó b = a √ó b (ordinary multiplication).
(i) Is ‚ąó closed on S?  (ii) Is ‚ąó associative on S?

*(Target: (i) Yes ‚ÄĒ check: (‚ąí1)√ó(‚ąí1)=1‚ąąS, (‚ąí1)√ó0=0‚ąąS, 1√ó1=1‚ąąS, etc. All products of elements from {‚ąí1,0,1} stay in {‚ąí1,0,1}. ‚úď (ii) Yes ‚ÄĒ multiplication of real numbers is associative.)*

**Q4.** TRUE or FALSE: If ‚ąó is a binary operation on S, then a ‚ąó b must equal b ‚ąó a for all a, b ‚ąą S.

*(Target: FALSE ‚ÄĒ commutativity is an optional property, not part of the definition. Matrix multiplication is a counterexample.)*

**[P55 ‚ÄĒ SCORE]**  Tally Q1‚ÄďQ4.

---

**[P76 ‚ÄĒ TRANSFER PROBE]**  (P76_MODE = Independence)

*Independence probe ‚ÄĒ self-contained.*

> Let S = {0, 1, 2, 3} and define a ‚äē b = (a + b) mod 4 (the remainder when a + b is divided by 4).
>
> (a) Verify that ‚äē is closed on S by computing 2 ‚äē 3 and 3 ‚äē 3.
> (b) Compute 1 ‚äē 3 and 3 ‚äē 1.  Is ‚äē commutative?
> (c) Find an element e ‚ąą S such that a ‚äē e = a for all a ‚ąą S.  (This is the identity element.)
> (d) For the element 3 ‚ąą S, find an element x such that 3 ‚äē x = e.  (This is the inverse of 3.)

*Expected answers:*
*(a) 2‚äē3 = 5 mod 4 = 1 ‚ąą S ‚úď; 3‚äē3 = 6 mod 4 = 2 ‚ąą S ‚úď.*
*(b) 1‚äē3 = 4 mod 4 = 0; 3‚äē1 = 0. Equal ‚ÄĒ commutative for this pair. (In general, yes: (a+b) mod 4 = (b+a) mod 4.)*
*(c) e = 0 ‚ÄĒ because a‚äē0 = (a+0) mod 4 = a for all a ‚ąą S.*
*(d) 3‚äē1 = 0 = e, so the inverse of 3 is 1.*

**[P55 ‚ÄĒ SCORE]**  Award 1 point for P76 if all four parts correct; 0 otherwise.

---

**[P75 ‚ÄĒ MASTERY ASSESSMENT]**

```
PASS_CRITERION: 5 out of 5 items (4 P77 + 1 P76)
THRESHOLD:      0.90  ‚Üí  ‚Ćą0.90 √ó 5‚ĆČ = 5
```

**[P55 ‚ÄĒ SCORE]**  Combine P77 tally + P76 score ‚Üí total out of 5.

**[P74 ‚ÄĒ ROUTING DECISION]**

- **Score = 5/5 ‚Üí MASTERY ACHIEVED.** Proceed to P78.
- **Score ‚Č§ 4/5 ‚Üí Identify which items failed:**
  - Q1 wrong ‚Üí closure gap ‚Üí TB-R01.
  - Q2 wrong ‚Üí MC-2 or MC-3 ‚Üí TB-R02 (matrix multiplication example).
  - Q3 wrong ‚Üí check (i) vs (ii) separately; closure gap ‚Üí TB-R01; associativity gap ‚Üí explain with numbers.
  - Q4 wrong ‚Üí MC-2 ‚Üí TB-R02 (commutativity is optional; give counterexample).
  - P76 wrong ‚Üí map error to (a)/(b)/(c)/(d): closure ‚Üí TB-R01; identity/inverse ‚Üí re-derive from definition; commutativity ‚Üí TB-R02.
  - After repair, re-administer only the failed item.

**[P55 ‚ÄĒ SCORE]**  Record repair outcome.

**[P78 ‚ÄĒ COMPLETION]**

```
BLUEPRINT_ID:    math.abst.binary-operation
MASTERY_REACHED: true
UNLOCKS:         math.abst.group-theory
NEXT_CONCEPT:    math.abst.group-theory
SESSION_CLOSE:   "You now understand that a binary operation is simply a closure-preserving
                  rule on a set. Commutativity, associativity, identity, and inverses are
                  extra properties an operation may have ‚ÄĒ and group theory is the study of
                  operations that have all four."
```

---

<!-- COMPONENT 5: PROTOCOL B (REPAIR) -->

## Component 5: Protocol B ‚ÄĒ Repair Chains

---

### TB-R01 ¬∑ Repair: CLOSURE-VIOLATION (MC-1)

**Trigger:** Student accepts an operation as binary despite the output leaving S on some pair.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Single counterexample suffices.**
> To DISPROVE closure, you only need ONE pair (a, b) where a ‚ąó b ‚ąČ S.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Structured check.**
> For the claimed operation and set, pick a "stress-test" pair:
> - If S ‚äÜ ‚Ą§ and ‚ąó = subtraction: try the smallest minus the largest.
> - If ‚ąó = division: try 1 √∑ 2.
> - If ‚ąó = square root: try ‚ąö2 (irrational, likely outside the set).

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Confirm the definition.**
> Binary operation: ‚ąó: S √ó S ‚Üí S ‚ÄĒ the OUTPUT must be IN S for EVERY pair. Not just most.

**Exit:** Return to the TA-A02 P49 checkpoint.

---

### TB-R02 ¬∑ Repair: COMMUTATIVITY-REQUIRED (MC-2)

**Trigger:** Student rejects an operation because a ‚ąó b ‚Č† b ‚ąó a.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ State what the definition requires.**
> The DEFINITION of binary operation only requires closure.
> The question "is it commutative?" is SEPARATE.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Counterexample: matrix multiplication.**
> Let A = [[1,1],[0,1]] and B = [[1,0],[1,1]].
> AB = [[2,1],[1,1]], BA = [[1,1],[1,2]].  AB ‚Č† BA.
> Yet matrix multiplication IS a binary operation on 2√ó2 real matrices ‚ÄĒ it is closed.
> It is simply NOT commutative.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Reframe commutativity.**
> Commutativity means "order doesn't matter."
> Binary operation means "closure."
> These are independent conditions.

**Exit:** Return to TA-A03 P49 checkpoint, part (ii).

---

### TB-R03 ¬∑ Repair: ARITHMETIC-ONLY (MC-3)

**Trigger:** Student does not recognise non-arithmetic rules as binary operations.

**Step 1 ‚ÄĒ Expand the example set.**
> Three non-arithmetic binary operations:
> 1. Set intersection ‚ą© on subsets of U: A ‚ą© B is always a subset of U ‚Üí closed.
> 2. String concatenation on strings: "hello" + "world" = "helloworld" ‚Üí still a string ‚Üí closed.
> 3. XOR on {0,1}: 0‚äē0=0, 0‚äē1=1, 1‚äē0=1, 1‚äē1=0 ‚Üí all in {0,1} ‚Üí closed.

**Step 2 ‚ÄĒ Apply the closure test to each.**
> For each one: "Does every pair of inputs produce an output in S?"
> If yes, it is a binary operation ‚ÄĒ regardless of how it looks.

**Step 3 ‚ÄĒ Restate the definition.**
> A binary operation is ANY rule ‚ąó: S√óS‚ÜíS.  The "‚ąó" can be anything.

**Exit:** Return to TA-A03 P49 checkpoint.

---

<!-- COMPONENT 6: P89 SPACED REPETITION -->

## Component 6: P89 Spaced Repetition

```
P89 SCHEDULE:
  Interval-1 (next session):
    Probe: "Is a ‚ąó b = 2^a a binary operation on ‚Ą§? Explain."
    Target: No ‚ÄĒ 2^a takes only one input (a), not two; or if interpreted as a‚ąób=2^(a+b),
            check closure on ‚Ą§: 2^(a+b) is always positive, so not in ‚Ą§ when result < 0
            is needed... actually 2^(anything) > 0, so 2^(a+b) ‚ąą ‚Ą§‚Āļ ‚äÜ ‚Ą§. But the
            function only depends on a, not b ‚ÄĒ it is not a genuine binary operation.
            PASS if student identifies the problem.
    Alternative probe: "Is subtraction a binary operation on ‚Ąē = {1,2,3,...}? Give a counterexample."
    Target: No ‚ÄĒ 2‚ąí5=‚ąí3‚ąČ‚Ąē. PASS if counterexample given.

  Interval-2 (+3 days):
    Probe: "Name one binary operation on ‚Ą§ that is NOT commutative."
    Target: Subtraction (3‚ąí5 ‚Č† 5‚ąí3). PASS if correct with justification.

  Interval-3 (+1 week):
    Probe: "Define a ‚ąó b = max(a,b) on ‚ĄĚ. Is ‚ąó a binary operation? Is it commutative?
            Does it have an identity element?"
    Target: Yes (closed, max(a,b)‚ąą‚ĄĚ). Commutative (max(a,b)=max(b,a)). No identity
            (there is no e ‚ąą ‚ĄĚ with max(a,e)=a for all a, since for a < e we get e ‚Č† a).
            PASS if all three answered correctly.

REACTIVATION_TRIGGER: Any probe fails ‚Üí requeue TA-A02 (closure check) and TA-A03 (contrast table).
```

---

<!-- COMPONENT 7: CROSS-BLUEPRINT DEPENDENCIES -->

## Component 7: Cross-Blueprint Dependencies

```
PREREQUISITES_CONSUMED:
  math.found.function-set-theoretic:
    Used in:   TA-A01 (binary op defined as function ‚ąó: S√óS‚ÜíS; arrow diagram),
               TA-A02 P64 (output must land in codomain = S),
               TA-A04 P76 (‚äē defined as a function on S={0,1,2,3}).
    Assumed:   Student can read f: A‚ÜíB notation; understands domain, codomain, Cartesian product.

UNLOCKS_ENABLED:
  math.abst.group-theory:
    Dependency: Group theory studies binary operations with ALL FOUR extra properties
                (closure, associativity, identity, inverses). Student must first hold the
                definition of binary operation and understand that the four properties are
                independent and optional.

CROSS_LINKS_NOTED:
  (none)
```

---

<!-- COMPONENT 8: TEACHING NOTES -->

## Component 8: Teaching Notes

**1. Closure is the ENTIRE definition; everything else is extra.**
Students typically arrive expecting a binary operation to be commutative, to have an identity, and
to behave like arithmetic.  The fastest way to break this expectation is TB-R02's matrix
multiplication example ‚ÄĒ two 2√ó2 matrices whose product depends on order.  Use it early if MC-2
appears.

**2. The set S matters as much as the rule.**
Subtraction on ‚Ą§: binary operation.  Subtraction on ‚Ąē: not a binary operation.  Same rule,
different set, different answer.  Emphasise this in TA-A02 to anchor MC-1 to the set, not just
the rule.

**3. The Cayley table is the clearest Pictorial representation for finite sets.**
For the P76 probe, a 4√ó4 Cayley table for ‚äē on {0,1,2,3} makes closure visible as "every entry
in the table is in {0,1,2,3}."  If a student struggles with parts (a)‚Äď(b), suggest building the
full table.

**4. bloom = understand: proofs not required, but justifications are.**
Students do not need to prove theorems about binary operations.  They need to explain why a given
rule is or is not a binary operation on a given set, and identify whether specific properties
hold, with one illustrative example or counterexample as evidence.

**5. This blueprint is a direct prerequisite for group theory (math.abst.group-theory).**
The group axioms (closure, associativity, identity, inverse) are exactly the four optional
properties introduced in TA-A03.  Point this out in P78 so students see the purpose of the
abstract definition they just learned.

---

GRAMMAR RULES

               TA-A01‚ÜíP03 ‚úď  TA-A02‚ÜíP41 ‚úď  TA-A03‚ÜíP06 ‚úď  TA-A04‚ÜíP91(P77) ‚úď
[PASS] V-11  GR-2: Every non-gate TA has P49.
               TA-A01‚ÜíP49 ‚úď  TA-A03‚ÜíP49 ‚úď  (TA-A02 is a gate TA; gate TAs exempt from GR-2 as
               the P64 + P49 structure serves the gate function)

               P77‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP76‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP75‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP74‚ÜíP55‚ÜíP78. ‚úď

[PASS] V-20  P74 routing covers all score outcomes (5/5 ‚Üí pass; ‚Č§4/5 ‚Üí item-specific repair).

CONTENT

             Four optional properties (associativity, commutativity, identity, inverse) named
             and explicitly labelled as optional.
             MC-1 (closure violation) anchored to the set-dependence of the definition.
             MC-2 (commutativity required) countered by matrix multiplication example.
             MC-3 (arithmetic-only) countered by set intersection, string concatenation, XOR.
             P76 probe exercises closure, commutativity, identity, and inverse on {0,1,2,3}
             with modular addition ‚ÄĒ no external Tier 1 dependency.

assets.title: Scaffolding Protocol
assets.content:
```
Entry ‚Üí TA-A01 (P03 concrete analogy + P11 representation shift + P49)
      ‚Üí TA-A02 (P41/P64 MC-1 gate: closure check + P49)
      ‚Üí TA-A03 (P06 contrast: closed vs not closed / commutative vs not + P49)
      ‚Üí TA-A04 (P91 terminal mastery gate)

Repair (Protocol B):
  MC-1 ‚Üí TB-R01 (closure re-anchoring)
  MC-2 ‚Üí TB-R02 (commutativity is optional)
  MC-3 ‚Üí TB-R03 (non-arithmetic examples)
```

---

<!-- COMPONENT 4: PROTOCOL A (MAIN) -->

assets.title: P89 Spaced Repetition
assets.content:
```
P89 SCHEDULE:
  Interval-1 (next session):
    Probe: "Is a ‚ąó b = 2^a a binary operation on ‚Ą§? Explain."
    Target: No ‚ÄĒ 2^a takes only one input (a), not two; or if interpreted as a‚ąób=2^(a+b),
            check closure on ‚Ą§: 2^(a+b) is always positive, so not in ‚Ą§ when result < 0
            is needed... actually 2^(anything) > 0, so 2^(a+b) ‚ąą ‚Ą§‚Āļ ‚äÜ ‚Ą§. But the
            function only depends on a, not b ‚ÄĒ it is not a genuine binary operation.
            PASS if student identifies the problem.
    Alternative probe: "Is subtraction a binary operation on ‚Ąē = {1,2,3,...}? Give a counterexample."
    Target: No ‚ÄĒ 2‚ąí5=‚ąí3‚ąČ‚Ąē. PASS if counterexample given.

  Interval-2 (+3 days):
    Probe: "Name one binary operation on ‚Ą§ that is NOT commutative."
    Target: Subtraction (3‚ąí5 ‚Č† 5‚ąí3). PASS if correct with justification.

  Interval-3 (+1 week):
    Probe: "Define a ‚ąó b = max(a,b) on ‚ĄĚ. Is ‚ąó a binary operation? Is it commutative?
            Does it have an identity element?"
    Target: Yes (closed, max(a,b)‚ąą‚ĄĚ). Commutative (max(a,b)=max(b,a)). No identity
            (there is no e ‚ąą ‚ĄĚ with max(a,e)=a for all a, since for a < e we get e ‚Č† a).
            PASS if all three answered correctly.

REACTIVATION_TRIGGER: Any probe fails ‚Üí requeue TA-A02 (closure check) and TA-A03 (contrast table).
```

<!-- COMPONENT 8: TEACHING NOTES -->
